title: The Cat's Sense of Smell: 200 Million Olfactory Receptors 🐱
description: Cats have a sense of smell 14 times more powerful than humans. Discover how the 200 million feline olfactory receptors and the Jacobson's organ work. Scientific data on the cat's nose and its sensory ability.

segments.voice: The sense of smell is the most dominant in cats. While humans rely mainly on sight, cats experience the world through their highly sophisticated noses. Their sense of smell is approximately 14 times more powerful than that of humans.
A cat's nose contains around 200 million specialized olfactory receptors. These receptors are distributed in the olfactory epithelium, a region in the nasal cavity where odors are processed.
For comparison, humans have approximately 5 million olfactory receptors. This means a cat can detect odors at much lower concentrations than we can, allowing them to smell food through closed cans.

In addition to normal olfactory receptors, cats have a special organ called the vomeronasal or Jacobson's organ. This organ is crucial for detecting pheromones, chemical substances released by other animals.
The Jacobson's organ is located on the palate, between the incisors. Cats can access this organ through a small duct called the vomeronasal duct.
When a cat lifts its nose and squints its eyes, making an expression known as the 'Flehmen response', it is using its Jacobson's organ. It is analyzing pheromones from other cats or the environment.
A study by the University of Pennsylvania in 2012 revealed that cats can detect changes in the Earth's magnetic field through their olfactory receptors. This could help them manage long distances.
A cat's olfactory ability is so developed that they can follow scent trails that are days old. They can distinguish between dozens of different smells simultaneously.
Cats can smell urine samples from other cats and determine their sex, age, health status, and reproductive availability. This is a complete communication system based purely on smell.

Researchers at the University of Tokyo trained cats to distinguish between urine samples from different individuals. The cats completed the task with 90% accuracy, demonstrating the sophistication of their sense of smell.
Each cat's nose has a unique pattern of ridges and creases, similar to a human fingerprint. This pattern, called a nasal print, could theoretically be used to identify individual cats.
The color of a cat's nose is also important. Cats with pink noses have different areas of olfactory sensitivity compared to cats with dark noses. This is due to pigmentation and vascularization.
segments.visual: cat pink nose black nose color

segments.voice: Cats can detect certain body odors in humans that indicate illness. There are reports of cats detecting cancer in their owners before any medical diagnosis.
A study published in 'Nature' in 2019 found that cats can detect subtle chemical changes in the human body. This could explain why cats are often drawn to sick people.
A cat's olfactory receptors are directly connected to the olfactory bulb in the brain, allowing for very rapid processing of olfactory information. This happens almost instantaneously.
Cats can detect the smell of catnip at concentrations as low as 1 part in 100 million. This is significantly more sensitive than the human ability to detect the smell of coffee or perfume.

When a cat rubs its face against you or objects, it is depositing its pheromones. This marks territory but is also a sign of affection. Its extremely sensitive sense of smell keeps these markers fresh.
In conclusion, a cat's sense of smell is one of its most extraordinary attributes. It's the reason your cat always knows exactly where you've been and what you've eaten, no matter how well you bathe.
